Three consecutive missed targets at a crucial time costMulwala's James Willettthe chance to win his first Olympic medal at the Paris 2024 Games.

Loginor signup to continue reading

Willett, 28, secured a place in the six-man final in the early hours of Wednesday morning, July 31,after shooting 123 of 125 targets in qualifying.

The slate was wiped clean for the remaining competitors, who were then ranked on their performance across the next 25 targets.

After making no mistakes with his first three attempts in the last stage of the two-day competition, he was unsuccessful with his next three attempts to drop him to sixth place.

Willett went on to miss a further three to record a score of 19 and was eliminated.

The bottom-ranked shooter after each of the next five targets then dropped until two remained, with 10 more shots to determine the winner.

Great Britain's Nathan Hales clinched the gold medal with a final score of 48, China's Qi Ying won silver on 44, while Guatemala's Jean Pierre Brol Cardenas took bronze on 35.

The other Australian competitor, Mitchell Iles, finished ninth on 122 targets, just failing to qualify for the final.

Willett's wife, Amy, toldThe Border Mailshe was "incredibly proud" of his efforts.

"Reaching this stage in such a competitive and high-pressure environment is remarkable," she said on Wednesday.

"As a spectator, it's both exciting and nerve-racking, it can be tough when things don't go according to his plan. This is the nature of the sport which can change the outcome in a matter of minutes.

"Missing three targets in the first six targets obviously wasn't what he wanted, but he always takes something positive to move forward when he next competes and did his best to try to regain his focus under such pressure.

"I have spoken to him, he is happy with how he went and truly trusts his performance. James indicated he did everything that he could, his resilience and positive outlook are great qualities of his."

Mrs Willett said her husband was the kind of person who took valuable lessons from every situation he's faced.

"This experience will undoubtedly make him a stronger and more determined competitor," she said.

"James is always looking at what's ahead, he is incredibly determined. This outcome only further pushes his desire to compete once again on the world stage in Los Angeles in 2028."

Willett placed fifth in the double trap at the Rio 2016 Olympicsand was 21stat theTokyo Games in 2021.

Mrs Willett thanked the Yarrawonga-Mulwala community for its support of Willett, particularly sponsors Kennedy Builders, Yarrawonga Toyota, Central Murray Credit Union, Hargraves Secured Investments, and Club Mulwala.

Beau Greenway is the Deputy Editor of The Border Mail. He likes to tell great stories of people in our communities. Email: beau.greenway@austcommunitymedia.com.au

title: Mulwala shooter James Willett narrowly misses medal at Paris Olympics | The Border Mail | Wodonga, VIC
description: Three early missed targets cost James Willett a medal at Paris 2024. His wife, Amy, was proud of his efforts and said he was determined to push for the 2028 Games.
